title: Buy Back Your Time vs Who Not How: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Dan Martell's Buy Back Your Time vs Dan Sullivan's Who Not How—two delegation books compared. Which will help you reclaim your time?

book1.title: Buy Back Your Time
book1.subtitle: Get Unstuck, Reclaim Your Freedom, and Build Your Empire
book1.author: Dan Martell
book1.description: Buy Back Your Time is Dan Martell's system for entrepreneurs to reclaim their time through strategic delegation and hiring. Martell introduces the Buyback Principle: for every dollar you earn, buy back one hour of your time. The book provides practical frameworks like the Replacement Ladder (what to delegate first), the Buyback Loop (audit, transfer, fill), and how to hire your first assistant. Martell shares his journey from overwhelmed founder to CEO with freedom. The focus is tactical—exactly how to delegate, what to delegate, and how to train people. It's about working less while growing your business more by investing in the right people.

book2.title: Who Not How
book2.subtitle: The Formula to Achieve Bigger Goals Through Accelerating Teamwork
book2.author: Dan Sullivan and Benjamin Hardy
book2.description: Who Not How presents a paradigm shift: instead of asking 'How do I achieve this goal?' ask 'Who can help me achieve this?' Dan Sullivan and Benjamin Hardy argue that trying to figure everything out yourself limits your growth. By finding the right people (the Whos) to handle tasks, you multiply your capabilities. The book covers mindset shifts, how to build relationships with Whos, and why collaboration beats solo effort. It's about thinking bigger by leveraging other people's skills and time. The concept applies to any area of life where you're stuck in 'how' mode instead of finding the right 'who.'

book1Strengths: The buyback loop of audit time, transfer tasks, and fill with  work creates clear system for buying back your time
At 256 pages with 3,800 ratings at 4.6 stars this is Martell's framework for entrepreneurs drowning in  hourly tasks
The replacement ladder showing how to progressively hire people to take over your work is tactical step by step roadmap
The pain line concept identifying the tipping point where doing everything yourself becomes unsustainable is relatable insight
The production quadrant showing time wasters versus money makers helps you ruthlessly cut low value activities immediately
Real examples from Martell's businesses show this working at scale giving you confidence the system actually works
The focus on buying back time to do  work not leisure reframes delegation as investment not laziness
book1Weaknesses: Only 3,800 ratings versus Who Not How's 5,800 shows it hasn't achieved the same mainstream traction yet
The framework assumes you're already making enough money to hire people which excludes bootstrapped solopreneurs struggling financially
At 256 pages some concepts feel repetitive when the core idea is simple delegate low value work to focus high value
The  work framing creates arbitrary threshold that doesn't account for different business models and profit margins
Martell's examples are from service businesses and agencies so product companies need to translate frameworks heavily
The emphasis on hiring employees versus contractors misses modern gig economy options that give more flexibility
book2Strengths: The who not how mindset shift from how do I do this to who can do this transforms how you approach every problem
At 256 pages with 5,800 ratings at 4.5 stars this is Dan Sullivan's framework taught in Strategic Coach for decades
The concept works at any budget level whether who is  virtual assistant or  specialist or equity partner
The freedom of focus showing how finding whos multiplies your impact exponentially is inspiring vision of leverage
The relationship focus teaching you to invest in whos as partners not just hired help creates better long term outcomes
The simple core concept makes it accessible to anyone while Buyback Your Time requires more business sophistication
The emphasis on procrastination being caused by asking how instead of who is psychologically insightful reframe
book2Weaknesses: At 256 pages it feels padded when the core concept could be explained in 50 page article or podcast episode
The framework assumes you can afford to hire whos which isn't realistic for early stage entrepreneurs burning savings
Some readers find the fable format with fictional characters annoying when they want straightforward business advice
The delegation advice feels obvious to anyone who's managed teams before so experienced leaders won't find much new
The lower 4.5 rating versus Buyback's 4.6 suggests readers found it less actionable despite having more followers
The book doesn't address how to find good whos or vet them which is actually the hard part of delegation

book1: For every dollar you earn, buy back one hour of your time.
The goal is not to work less. The goal is to do more of what you love.
Delegation is not about dumping tasks. It's about designing systems.
Your time is your most valuable asset. Treat it that way.
Most entrepreneurs are their own biggest bottleneck.
Freedom comes from building a business that doesn't need you.
book2: Stop asking 'How do I do this?' Start asking 'Who can do this?'
Who Not How is about abundance, not scarcity.
You can have anything you want if you know the right Who.
Freedom comes from having the right Whos in your life.
The quality of your life is determined by the Whos in your life.
Trying to figure out how limits your thinking. Finding the right who expands it.

book1: You're overwhelmed and need specific tactics for delegating
You want step-by-step frameworks for hiring and training
You're an entrepreneur ready to hire your first assistant
You need practical templates and scripts, not just philosophy
You want recent strategies for building remote teams
book2: You need a fundamental mindset shift about achievement
You're stuck thinking you have to figure everything out yourself
You want to think bigger but don't know how
You need permission to ask for help and collaborate
You're interested in Strategic Coach principles

verdict.summary: Buy Back Your Time wins decisively for ACTION (4.7 stars, 3,200 ratings vs 4.6 stars, 5,800 ratings). Dan Martell gives you frameworks you can use Monday—Replacement Ladder, Buyback Loop, DRIP method, Camcorder training. Who Not How gives you philosophy—'ask who not how.' That's ONE insight repeated 256 pages. Most entrepreneurs need tactical systems more than mindset shifts. Buy Back Your Time is newer (2023 vs 2020), more detailed, and more useful.
verdict.recommendation: Read Buy Back Your Time first—it's the complete system. You'll get the Replacement Ladder (delegate admin $10/hr → delivery $25/hr → marketing $50/hr → sales $100/hr, in that order), the Buyback Loop (Audit with DRIP method → Transfer with Camcorder training → Fill with high-value work), the Buyback Rate formula (Annual Income ÷ 2,000 hours = your hourly rate, delegate everything below it), and the Pain Line concept (track energy, delegate draining tasks). Dan Martell built 5 SaaS companies with this. It's PROVEN. 288 pages, published 2023, modern tactics for hiring Philippines VAs ($5-15/hr) and remote teams. THEN read Who Not How ONLY if you need the mindset unlock—Dan Sullivan's 'stop asking how, start asking who' reframe is powerful if you're stuck in 'I must do everything myself' mode. The book gives PERMISSION to collaborate and leverage relationships. But it's light on tactics. You'll get inspired but not a playbook. If you're broke or early-stage: Who Not How works without money (find partners, mentors, collaborators). If you have $500-1000/month budget: Buy Back Your Time shows you EXACTLY how to delegate. Most entrepreneurs are past the mindset phase—they KNOW they should delegate, they just don't know HOW. That's why Buy Back Your Time wins.
